diagrams.title: Transaction Life Cycle Flow
diagrams.description: A flowchart showing the complete lifecycle of a database transaction from initiation to completion
elements: Start Node (Transaction Begin) → Decision Diamond (Operation Success?) → Two paths: Success Path leads to Commit, Failure Path leads to Rollback → End Node
States shown: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Aborted
Arrows with labels: execute SQL, commit, rollback, network failure, system crash
Color coding: Green for success path, Red for failure/rollback path
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete flow of transaction processing including success and failure scenarios, and learn when commit versus rollback is appropriate

diagrams.title: ACID Properties Architecture
diagrams.description: Block diagram showing how each ACID property contributes to transaction integrity
elements: Central box: 'Database Transaction'
Four surrounding boxes connected to center: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (valid state), Isolation (concurrent execution), Durability (permanent results)
Each property box contains a real-world example icon
Arrows show relationships between properties
Bottom section shows how JDBC methods enforce each property
diagrams.purpose: Students visualize how ACID properties work together to ensure reliable database operations and which JDBC methods implement each property

diagrams.title: JDBC Transaction Management Architecture
diagrams.description: Class diagram showing JDBC objects and their transaction-related methods
elements: Connection interface box with methods: setAutoCommit(boolean), commit(), rollback(), setSavepoint(), setTransactionIsolation(int)
Statement interface box below Connection
Transaction state box showing: Auto-commit ON/OFF modes
Visual arrows showing data flow from Application → Connection → Statement → Database
Insets showing code snippets for each method call
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the JDBC API structure for transaction management and which methods to use for different transaction scenarios

diagrams.title: Bank Transfer Transaction Sequence
diagrams.description: Sequence diagram showing a money transfer between two accounts with transaction handling
elements: Vertical swimlanes: Java Application, Connection, Database
Horizontal arrows: 1) setAutoCommit(false), 2) UPDATE account_a SET balance = balance - 1000, 3) UPDATE account_b SET balance = balance + 1000, 4) commit()
Failure scenario path shown with dashed lines: exception occurs → rollback()
Timeline showing order of operations
Success and failure outcome boxes at bottom
diagrams.purpose: Students apply transaction concepts to a real banking scenario and see exactly where commit and rollback are called in a practical context

diagrams.title: Savepoint Transaction Flow
diagrams.description: Flow diagram showing nested transactions using savepoints for partial rollbacks
elements: Main transaction starts at top
Savepoint nodes shown as diamond shapes: Savepoint1 after first operation, Savepoint2 after second operation
Three SQL operation boxes between savepoints
Branching paths: If operation 2 fails → rollback to Savepoint1 → continue with operation 3 → commit
If operation 3 fails → rollback to Savepoint2 only operation 3 is undone
Legend showing: rollback to savepoint vs full rollback
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how to implement fine-grained transaction control using savepoints for partial rollbacks in complex scenarios

charts.title: JDBC Transaction Methods Comparison
charts.content: Table with columns: Method Name, Purpose, Parameters, Return Type, Example Use Case. Rows: setAutoCommit(boolean autoCommit) - enables/disables auto-commit mode, commit() - makes all changes permanent, rollback() - undoes all changes, rollback(Savepoint sp) - undoes changes to savepoint, setTransactionIsolation(int level) - sets isolation level, setSavepoint() - creates unnamed savepoint, setSavepoint(String name) - creates named savepoint

charts.title: Transaction State Transition Diagram
charts.content: Flowchart showing states: Active (executing SQL) → Partially Committed (after last SQL, before commit) → Committed (permanent) OR Aborted (rollback). Also shows Active → Aborted on rollback. Arrows labeled with events causing transitions: execute SQL, commit, rollback, system failure

charts.title: Isolation Levels Comparison
charts.content: Table with rows for each isolation level: TRANSACTION_READ_UNCOMMITTED (allows dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, phantom reads), TRANSACTION_READ_COMMITTED (prevents dirty reads), TRANSACTION_REPEATABLE_READ (prevents dirty and non-repeatable reads), TRANSACTION_SERIALIZABLE (prevents all three). Columns: Level Constant, Dirty Read, Non-Repeatable Read, Phantom Read, Performance Impact

charts.title: Auto-Commit Mode Decision Flow
charts.content: Decision flowchart: Start → Should multiple operations be atomic? → If YES: setAutoCommit(false) → Execute all operations → Check for errors → If error: rollback, If success: commit. If NO: setAutoCommit(true) or keep default → Each operation automatically committed → End. Shows when to use manual vs auto-commit mode

branches: Isolation Levels (Read Uncommitted, Read Committed, Repeatable Read, Serializable)
Error Handling (try-catch with rollback, automatic rollback on exceptions)
Real-World Applications (Banking transfers, Order processing, Inventory management)
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting transaction concepts, JDBC API methods, transaction states, and practical applications for exam preparation and concept reinforcement

branches: Auto-Commit Mode (default true, manual control needed for transactions)
Commit Operation (makes changes permanent in database)
Savepoints (enable partial rollback in complex transactions)
mindMaps.purpose: Focused mind map on JDBC-specific transaction management showing how the Connection interface provides all transaction control capabilities and when to use each feature
